exercises.title: Prose as wishes, constraints as tests
exercises.prompt: Instructions written as prose are wishes; instructions written as constraints are tests. Explain the difference, why prose rules get ignored, and the separation of routing prose from operational rules.
exercises.hint: Prose: 'please stay in scope' — the model may or may not comply, and you can't check. Constraint: a machine-checkable rule (allowed files = X) that an agent checks at runtime and a reviewer verifies after. Prose ignored because it's not enforced. Routing prose (where to find things) is separate from operational rules (what to do/not do).
exercises.solution:
**Prose (wishes) vs constraints (tests):**
- **Prose instructions are wishes:** 'please stay in scope', 'be careful with the database', 'make sure it's correct' — these are **hopes that the model will comply**. The model **may or may not follow them** (it's stochastic and can ignore/forget instructions, Phase 11.1), and crucially **you can't *check* whether it complied** — there's no way to verify 'did it stay in scope?' from a prose wish. Prose is **unenforceable and unverifiable.**
- **Constraints are tests:** a constraint is a **machine-checkable rule** — 'allowed files: [X, Y]; forbidden: [Z]', 'definition of done: tests pass AND no files outside scope changed'. A constraint is something **the agent can check at runtime** (before acting: 'is this file in the allowed set?') **AND a reviewer/verifier can verify after the fact** ('did the diff only touch allowed files?'). A constraint is **enforceable and verifiable** — it's a *test*, not a wish.
The shift: **turn each rule from prose (a wish) into a constraint (a test)** — something checkable at runtime and verifiable after. 'Instructions written as prose are wishes; instructions written as constraints are tests.'

**Why prose rules get ignored:** prose rules are ignored because **nothing enforces them:**
- **The model is stochastic and instruction-following is imperfect** (Phase 11.1) — it may **not attend to, may forget, or may override** a prose instruction (especially in a long context, buried among other text, or when the task pulls it elsewhere — scope creep, Lesson 26). A prose 'stay in scope' competes with everything else in the context and often loses.
- **There's no check** — even if the model violates a prose rule, **nothing catches it** (you can't verify compliance with a wish), so violations go unnoticed and unship-blocked. 
- **No consequence** — a prose wish has no teeth; a constraint has a *check* that fails if violated.
So prose rules are **best-effort behavioral hints the model may ignore, with no enforcement** — which is exactly why agents drift, exceed scope, and violate 'rules' (the structural failure modes, Lesson 26). To actually enforce a rule, it must become a **constraint** (checkable, verifiable).

**Separation of routing prose from operational rules:** the workbench **separates two kinds of instruction:**
- **Routing prose:** **where to find things** — 'the scope contract is at X, the state file is at Y, run tests with Z' (the root router, Lesson 32). This is **navigational/informational** prose — it's fine as prose because it's just **pointing to resources** (the agent follows the pointer). Prose is appropriate for *routing*.
- **Operational rules:** **what to do and what not to do** — startup rules, forbidden actions, definition of done, approval boundaries. These are **behavioral rules that must be *enforced*** — so they should be **constraints (machine-checkable), not prose.** Prose is wrong for *operational rules* (they get ignored).
**Why separate them:** routing (where things are) can stay prose (it's navigational, low-stakes); but **operational rules (what the agent must/mustn't do) need to be constraints** (enforceable, verifiable) because **they're the rules that matter for reliability** (staying in scope, not doing forbidden things, knowing when done). Mixing them — burying operational rules in routing prose — means the **important rules get treated as ignorable wishes.** Separating them means **routing stays light prose, operational rules become hard constraints** (checked at runtime, verified after). The separation ensures the **rules that determine reliability are enforced (constraints), not hoped for (prose).** This is the core move of this lesson: **operational rules → executable constraints** (next exercise).

exercises.title: Expressing rules as machine-checkable constraints
exercises.prompt: Express the key operational rules as machine-checkable constraints: startup rules, forbidden actions, definition of done, uncertainty handling, approval boundaries. Implement a rule checker that scores a run against the rule set. Give an example of each rule type as a constraint.
exercises.hint: Startup: must read state/init before acting. Forbidden: must not touch files X, must not run command Y. Definition of done: tests pass AND scope respected. Uncertainty: must flag/ask when confidence low. Approval: must get human OK for irreversible actions. Rule checker: evaluate each rule against the run, score pass/fail.
exercises.solution:
**The key operational rules as machine-checkable constraints:**
- **Startup rules:** 'must read the state file and run init before acting' → **constraint:** *check that the run's first actions include reading state / running init.* (Verifiable: did it read state before doing work?)
- **Forbidden actions:** 'must not modify production config / must not run destructive commands' → **constraint:** *check the run's actions/diff for any forbidden file changes or forbidden commands.* (Verifiable: did it touch a forbidden file or run a forbidden command?)
- **Definition of done:** 'done means: tests pass AND no out-of-scope files changed AND the acceptance criteria met' → **constraint:** *check tests pass, diff is in-scope, criteria satisfied.* (Verifiable: is the work *actually* complete by these checks, not by the agent's say-so?)
- **Uncertainty handling:** 'when confidence is low / information is missing, must flag it / ask rather than guess' → **constraint:** *check that uncertain situations triggered a flag/question rather than a fabricated action.* (Verifiable: did it guess when it should have asked?)
- **Approval boundaries:** 'irreversible/high-stakes actions require human approval' → **constraint:** *check that any high-stakes action was preceded by an approval (human-in-the-loop, Phase 11.9).* (Verifiable: did it take an irreversible action without approval?)
Each operational rule becomes a **checkable predicate over the run's artifacts** (actions, diff, state, outputs) — a *test*, not a wish.

**A rule checker:**
```python
def check_rules(run, rules):
    results = []
    for rule in rules:
        passed = rule["check"](run)              # evaluate the constraint over the run
        results.append({"rule": rule["name"], "passed": passed})
    score = sum(r["passed"] for r in results) / len(results)
    return {"score": score, "results": results,
            "violations": [r["rule"] for r in results if not r["passed"]]}

RULES = [
  {"name": "startup", "check": lambda run: "read_state" in run.early_actions},
  {"name": "forbidden_files",
   "check": lambda run: not any(f in FORBIDDEN for f in run.changed_files)},
  {"name": "definition_of_done",
   "check": lambda run: run.tests_passed and run.scope_respected},
  {"name": "uncertainty",
   "check": lambda run: run.guessed_on_missing_info is False},
  {"name": "approval",
   "check": lambda run: all(a.approved for a in run.high_stakes_actions)},
]
```
The rule checker **evaluates each constraint against the run** (its actions, changed files, test results, etc.), **scores pass/fail per rule**, and **reports violations.** This produces a **rule report** — an **objective, machine-checkable assessment** of whether the run followed the operational rules (vs hoping it did). The report feeds into the **verification gate** (Lesson 38) and the **reviewer** (Lesson 39) — it's a concrete artifact, not a vibe.

**Example of each rule type as a constraint:**
- **Startup:** `"read_state" in run.early_actions` — did it read state before acting?
- **Forbidden:** `no forbidden file in run.changed_files` — did it avoid forbidden files?
- **Definition of done:** `run.tests_passed and run.scope_respected` — is it *actually* done (tests pass + in scope)?
- **Uncertainty:** `not run.guessed_on_missing_info` — did it flag/ask instead of guessing?
- **Approval:** `all high-stakes actions approved` — did it get approval for irreversible actions?
Each is a **boolean check over the run's artifacts** — turning the operational rules into **automated tests** the run is scored against. This is the lesson's core: **operational rules become executable constraints, checked by a rule checker, producing an objective rule report** — 'instructions as tests, not wishes.' The agent can check these *at runtime* (before acting) and the verification gate/reviewer check them *after* (Lessons 38-39) — enforcement and verification, not hope.
exercises.starter_code:
def check_rules(run, rules):
    # Evaluate each rule['check'](run); return {'score' (fraction passed),
    # 'results', 'violations' (names of failed rules)}.
    pass

tests.desc: a failing rule is a violation
the score is the pass fraction

exercises.title: Why executable constraints work, runtime vs post-hoc checking, and the constraint discipline
exercises.prompt: Explain why executable constraints succeed where prose fails (enforcement + verification), the dual role of constraints (the agent checks at runtime, the reviewer verifies after), how this connects to the failure taxonomy (Lesson 26) and verification gates (Lesson 38), and the limits (not everything is machine-checkable; some rules need a reviewer agent).
exercises.hint: Constraints succeed because they're enforced (agent checks before acting) AND verified (checker/reviewer checks after) — prose is neither. Dual role: runtime self-check (prevent) + post-hoc verification (catch). Connects to failure modes (constraints counter scope creep etc.) and verification gates (the gate runs the rule checker). Limits: subjective/semantic rules aren't fully machine-checkable — need an LLM reviewer.
exercises.solution:
**Why executable constraints succeed where prose fails:** the difference is **enforcement + verification:**
- **Prose fails because it's neither enforced nor verified** (Exercise 1) — the model may ignore it, and you can't check compliance. A wish.
- **Constraints succeed because they're BOTH enforced and verified:**
  - **Enforced (at runtime):** the agent can **check the constraint *before* acting** ('is this file in the allowed set? — if not, don't touch it') — so the constraint **prevents** violations proactively. 
  - **Verified (after the fact):** a rule checker / reviewer can **check the constraint *after*** ('did the diff only touch allowed files?') — so violations are **caught** if they slip through, before the work ships.
A constraint is a **test** — and tests work because they're **objective, automated, and have teeth** (a failing test blocks shipping). Turning a rule into a constraint gives it **enforcement (runtime check) and verification (post-hoc check)** that prose lacks — which is *why* it works. It's the same reason **tests beat 'please write correct code'** (Phase 11.1's prompts-are-code, Lesson 19's test-gating): objective, checkable, enforced.

**The dual role of constraints (runtime self-check + post-hoc verification):** constraints serve **two distinct enforcement points:**
1. **Runtime self-check (prevention):** the **agent checks the constraint *during* the run, before acting** — 'is this action allowed by the rules?' — so it **avoids violations proactively** (don't touch a forbidden file, don't take an unapproved irreversible action). This **prevents** failures before they happen (the agent self-governs against the constraints).
2. **Post-hoc verification (detection):** the **rule checker / verification gate / reviewer checks the constraint *after* the run** — 'did the run actually comply?' — so violations that **slipped past the runtime check are caught** before the work is accepted (the verification gate, Lesson 38; the reviewer, Lesson 39). This **detects** failures the prevention missed.
The **dual role** is defense-in-depth: **prevent at runtime (the agent self-checks) AND detect after (the verifier checks)** — so a violation must escape *both* the agent's self-check and the post-hoc verification to ship. (And crucially, the **post-hoc verification doesn't trust the agent's self-check** — the verifier independently confirms, because the agent can't reliably grade itself, Lesson 5. The runtime self-check helps but isn't trusted as sufficient.)

**Connection to the failure taxonomy (Lesson 26) and verification gates (Lesson 38):**
- **Failure taxonomy (Lesson 26):** executable constraints **directly counter the structural failure modes** — 'forbidden files' constraints counter **scope creep**, 'definition of done' constraints counter **task-verification failures** (false 'done'), 'uncertainty handling' counters **hallucinated actions** (guess instead of ask), 'approval boundaries' counter unauthorized dangerous actions. The constraints are the **machine-checkable encoding of the rules that prevent the failure modes** — turning 'don't do these failures' from a wish into enforced+verified tests.
- **Verification gates (Lesson 38):** the **verification gate *runs the rule checker*** (Exercise 2's rule report) as part of its verdict — the gate combines the **rule report (these constraints)** with the scope report, feedback, and diff to decide 'is this task actually done?' (Lesson 38). So **executable constraints feed the verification gate** — the constraints are the *rules*; the gate *enforces* them (along with scope and verification) before accepting the work. Constraints (this lesson) + verification gates (Lesson 38) are the **rule-enforcement half of the workbench.**

**The limits (not everything is machine-checkable):** executable constraints work for **objectively checkable rules** (file allowlists, test results, command checks, approval presence) — but **not everything is machine-checkable:**
- **Subjective/semantic rules** — 'the code should be clean and well-designed', 'the response should be helpful and appropriate', 'the change should match the codebase's conventions' — these are **judgments, not boolean checks.** A simple rule checker **can't verify them** (there's no deterministic test for 'is this well-designed?').
- **For these, you need a reviewer agent (Lesson 39)** — an **LLM-based reviewer** that can **make the judgment** (evaluate clean design, appropriateness, convention-matching) where a deterministic checker can't. The reviewer is the **semantic/subjective verification** layer that complements the **deterministic constraint checker** (Exercise 2). 
So the verification stack is: **deterministic executable constraints (this lesson, Lesson 38) for objectively-checkable rules + an LLM reviewer agent (Lesson 39) for subjective/semantic rules.** You **express every rule you *can* as a machine-checkable constraint** (most operational rules — scope, forbidden actions, definition of done, approval), and use the **reviewer agent for the rules that require judgment** (design quality, appropriateness). The constraint discipline: **make as much as possible objectively checkable (constraints), and use the reviewer for the irreducibly subjective remainder.** The synthesis: **instructions become reliable only when turned from prose (wishes — ignored, unverifiable) into executable constraints (tests — enforced at runtime, verified after)** — which succeed because they have **enforcement + verification** that prose lacks. Constraints play a **dual role** (runtime prevention + post-hoc detection), **counter the structural failure modes** (Lesson 26), and **feed the verification gate** (Lesson 38). The limit is that **subjective rules need an LLM reviewer** (Lesson 39) — so the workbench combines **deterministic constraints (objective rules) + reviewer agent (subjective rules)** for complete rule enforcement. 'Instructions as executable constraints' is the move that makes the workbench's rules *actually enforced* rather than hoped for — the foundation of reliable agent behavior.
